title: 2017-18 & 18-19 CNA Comprehensive Program Review Two year cycle (Part 1)
text:
Comprehensive Program Review Two-Year Cycle
Certified Nurse Assistant
Rhonda Brown, DSD Faculty
Cora Brownell, Director
December 2018
I. Description of the Program: this course teaches state approved CNA curriculum
that includes theory, skills lab and clinical experience to acquire the necessary skills
and knowledge for the state certification exam.

to be able to seek entry level employment in the health care field.
c) Whom we serve include demographics
The program serves the local community of Siskiyou and Shasta counties.

title: 2017-18 & 18-19 CNA Comprehensive Program Review Two year cycle (Part 2)
text:
II. External Factors with Significant Impact
a) Budgetary constraints or opportunities: Arthur N. Rupe Grant (RUPE) supports
with student expenses. The dollar amount of the grant varies from year to year.
b) Competition from other institutions: Shasta College offers a CNA program.
c) Requirements for four -year institutions: N/A
d) Institutional regulation, policies, standards, and other mandates:
CDPH faculty minimum requirements impact our ability to hire qualified
instructors in the past with clinical experience in a skilled nursing facility for at
least two years of dir4ect patient care. The regulation was amended this year to
include other clinical nursing experience to qualify faculty.
e) Non-Institutional regulation, policies, standards, and other mandates:
• Clinical site partners do not charge fees for our students practicing in their
facilities.
f) Job market
• Requirements of prospective employers: CNA certificate in good standing.
• Development in field: High need for this level of health care nationwide.
III. Progress on Outcomes Assessment:
Program Outcomes
Upon completion of this course, the student should be able to:
1. Summarize hospitals functions and the roles and responsibilities of the Certified Nurse Assistant
within the hospital.
2. Evaluate the personal and interpersonal skills necessary in the provision of care for the ill and the
aged.
3. Use appropriate protocol to prevent accidents and injury to both resident and care provider and
participate in the management of emergencies and unusual occurrences.
4. Identify 5 patient rights specific to residents in a long term care facility, and provide examples of
violations of such rights.
5. Identify and demonstrate guidelines and precautions to use when lifting and moving the resident.
6. Apply the principles of infection control, utilizing Universal Precautions to reduce the
transmission of infectious agents.
7. Compare and contrast the units of measurement in health care.
8. Assess and implement the personal care needs of a variety of residents utilizing the established
plan of care.

-- 3 of 6 --

title: 2017-18 & 18-19 CNA Comprehensive Program Review Two year cycle (Part 3)
text:
9. Select the correct methodology in performing supportive patient care procedures.
10. Differentiate between normal and abnormal vital signs and factors that affect the normal ranges.
11. Define a well-balanced diet and assess the resident’s need for adjustments in meal type.
12. Initiate immediate and temporary intervention when identifying signs and symptoms of distress in
the resident.
13. Construct basic care procedures focused on the common health disorders frequently found in long
term care, noting the psychological, social, cultural, and recreational needs accompanying those
disorders.
14. Define the 5 types of rehabilitative therapy commonly used in long term care facilities and the
impact each can have in promoting the resident’s potential.
15. Observe and report changes in the resident’s physical and emotional condition and document
these findings according to legal requirements/guidelines.
16. Differentiate the psychological and cultural aspects of caring for the chronically/terminally ill
resident and meet the needs of the resident’s family.

IV. Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators:
SEE GRAPH UNDER I(g).
c.) FT/PT Faculty Ratio/Impact on Student Success: Most cohorts are scheduled in
summer session. Adjunct faculty teaches the course.
V. Other Unit-Specific Quantitative/Qualitative Research
N/A
VI. Evaluation
a) Representativeness of population served see I (e)
b) Alternative modes and schedules of delivery: Canvas augmented classes. Hi fidelity
skills/simulation lab
Partnerships: COMMUNITY CLINICAL SITES; Shasta View Estates Skilled Nursing
Facility.
c) Innovation and Implementation of best practices: Clinical skills lab hours were added
to the curriculum Spring 2018.
d) Efficiency in operations:. Due to requests from local agencies, COS Nursing began
building up the Certified Nursing Assistant Program to have admissions both in spring
and fall on the main Yreka campus, starting in spring 2018. At the same time, a second
section in both spring and summer in Alturas in Modoc County dropped away when the
Modoc hospital achieved California Department of Public Health approval to run the
course on its own. Student completion in the combined sections had reached an all-time
high of 23 graduates in summer 2017, however once Modoc discontinued with COS, the
spring 2018 student completion total dropped to 17. Lower enrollment impacts the
efficiency of operations. There is a fine balance in student numbers for CNA since
higher enrollment requires more adjunct clinical instructors.
e) Efficiency in resource use: We continue to evaluate resource allocation whether it be
staffing or supplies to improve the nursing cost effectiveness.
f) Staffing

-- 5 of 6 --

Participation in shared governance:
a) Professional development and training: Faculty are afforded additional on campus and out of
town trainings.
a) Compliance with applicable mandates: All faculty comply with BRN licensing CPR,
and immunization requirements.
I. Vision
Continue to offer an excellent CNA program to meet the needs of
our community and partners.
II. Progress on Prior Goals: The new CNA curriculum was launched
In Spring 18. The program was re-evaluated in summer 2018 and appropriate changes were made to
meet the need of the students and to keep current with trends and evidenced-based best practice standards.
III. Two-year Action Plan:
• Continue to deliver a quality rigorous academic and clinical nursing program to produce excellent
new CNA graduates..
• . Continue to evaluate students’ needs and nursing budget dollar allocation for continued student
success.
We the undersigned certify that the LVN-RN Step-Up Program, program review process included
broad dialogue about student learning, program effectiveness and resource allocation.
Rhonda Brown Principal Preparer
Cora Brownell Contributor
